expanded_description: Learn to identify, predict, and write single replacement (displacement) reactions where one element replaces another in a compound. Master the activity series for metals and halogens to predict reaction spontaneity and products.

core_concept.principle: More Reactive Elements Displace Less Reactive Elements
core_concept.explanation: Single replacement reactions occur when a more reactive element displaces a less reactive element from a compound. The general pattern is A + BC → AC + B, where A is a more reactive element than B. These reactions are governed by the activity series—a ranking of elements by their tendency to lose electrons (metals) or gain electrons (halogens). Understanding reactivity trends enables accurate prediction of whether a reaction will occur and what products will form.

image.alt: Diagram showing element A displacing B from compound BC to form AC and free B
image.caption: Figure 7.7.1: Single replacement reactions follow A + BC → AC + B. The more reactive element (A) displaces the less reactive element (B) from its compound.

sections.section_title: What Is a Single Replacement Reaction?
sections.introduction: Single replacement reactions are redox processes where one element 'kicks out' another from a compound. These reactions are fundamental to corrosion, metal extraction, and many industrial processes.

key_concept.definition: A single replacement (displacement) reaction is a chemical reaction in which one element replaces another element in a compound. The general equation is: A + BC → AC + B, where A is a more reactive element than B.
key_concept.context: These reactions are always redox reactions: the replacing element is oxidized (loses electrons) while the displaced element is reduced (gains electrons).

components.interpretation: A represents a pure element (metal or halogen); BC represents a compound; A replaces B in the compound, releasing B as a free element. Reaction occurs only if A is more reactive than B.

image.alt: Visual representation of element A replacing B in compound BC
image.caption: Figure 7.7.2: Element A must be more reactive than B for the reaction to proceed spontaneously.

characteristics: A and B are metals; C is typically an anion (Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻, etc.)
Reaction occurs only if A is above B in the activity series
Often occurs in aqueous solution
May produce visible metal deposition or color change
classifications.behavior: More active metal loses electrons (oxidized) to form cation; less active metal cation gains electrons (reduced) to form elemental metal.

examples.explanation: Zinc (more active) displaces copper from copper(II) sulfate. Blue solution fades; reddish copper metal deposits on zinc.

image.caption: Zinc displaces copper; color change and metal deposition confirm reaction.

examples.explanation: Magnesium displaces silver from silver nitrate. Silvery magnesium dissolves; gray silver crystals form.

image.caption: Active magnesium displaces less active silver.

examples.explanation: Copper is below zinc in activity series; cannot displace zinc. Solution remains blue; no visible change.

image.caption: Less active metal cannot displace more active metal.

characteristics: Metal reacts with acid to produce hydrogen gas
Reaction occurs only if metal is above H in activity series
Bubbles of H₂ gas are visible evidence
Reaction rate depends on metal reactivity and acid concentration
classifications.behavior: Active metal loses electrons to H⁺ ions; H⁺ gains electrons to form H₂ gas; metal forms cation that pairs with acid anion.

examples.explanation: Magnesium reacts vigorously with hydrochloric acid; rapid H₂ bubbling; magnesium dissolves.

image.caption: Active metal + acid → salt + hydrogen gas.

examples.explanation: Zinc reacts with sulfuric acid; moderate bubbling; common lab preparation of hydrogen.

image.caption: Zinc displaces hydrogen from acid.

examples.explanation: Copper is below hydrogen in activity series; cannot displace H⁺ from acid. No bubbles observed.

image.caption: Less active metals don't react with acids.

classifications.value: A (very active metal) + H₂O → AO/Hydroxide + H₂(g)
characteristics: Only most active metals (Group 1, some Group 2) react with cold water
Less active metals may react with steam
Reaction produces metal hydroxide/oxide and hydrogen gas
Often highly exothermic and vigorous
classifications.behavior: Very active metals lose electrons to water molecules; water is reduced to H₂ gas; metal forms hydroxide or oxide.

examples.explanation: Sodium reacts violently with water; melts into sphere, darts on surface, ignites H₂. Extreme caution required.

image.caption: Group 1 metals react vigorously with water.

examples.explanation: Magnesium doesn't react with cold water but reacts with steam to form magnesium oxide and hydrogen.

image.caption: Less active metals require steam for water reaction.

examples.explanation: Iron doesn't react with cold or hot water; reacts slowly with steam at very high temperatures.

image.caption: Moderately active metals don't react with water.

characteristics: X and B are halogens (F, Cl, Br, I); Y is typically a metal cation
Reaction occurs only if X is above B in halogen activity series
Often observed as color change in solution
Fluorine is too reactive for aqueous displacement reactions
classifications.behavior: More reactive halogen gains electrons less readily (is stronger oxidizing agent); displaces less reactive halogen from its salt.

examples.explanation: Chlorine (more reactive) displaces bromine from sodium bromide. Colorless → orange/brown as Br₂ forms.

image.caption: Color change indicates halogen displacement.

examples.explanation: Bromine displaces iodine from potassium iodide. Orange → brown/purple as I₂ forms (may appear as dark solid).

image.caption: Halogen reactivity decreases down group.

examples.explanation: Iodine is least reactive halogen; cannot displace chlorine from its salt. No color change observed.

image.caption: Less reactive halogen cannot displace more reactive one.

principles.statement: Every single replacement reaction involves electron transfer: the replacing element is oxidized (loses electrons), the displaced element is reduced (gains electrons).
principles.analogy: Like a relay race: one runner (element) passes the baton (electrons) to another; the passer slows down (oxidized), the receiver speeds up (reduced).

principles.statement: A single replacement reaction occurs spontaneously only if the free element is more reactive than the element it would replace in the compound.
principles.application: Zn + CuSO₄ → reaction (Zn more active than Cu); Cu + ZnSO₄ → no reaction (Cu less active than Zn).
principles.example: This principle allows prediction of reaction outcomes without experimentation, using the activity series as a reference.

sections.section_title: The Activity Series: Predicting Reaction Outcomes
sections.introduction: The activity series is a powerful predictive tool that ranks elements by their tendency to undergo oxidation (metals) or reduction (halogens). Mastering this series enables accurate prediction of single replacement reactions.

key_concept.definition: The activity series is a list of elements arranged in order of decreasing reactivity. For metals, reactivity is the tendency to lose electrons and form cations; for halogens, reactivity is the tendency to gain electrons and form anions.
key_concept.context: An element can displace any element below it in the activity series from a compound, but cannot displace elements above it.

categories.description: Metals are ranked by their tendency to lose electrons (oxidize). Higher metals displace lower metals from compounds.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Reactivity correlates with ionization energy and electrode potential: metals with low ionization energy and negative reduction potentials are more reactive.

examples.process: Top tier: Li > K > Ba > Ca > Na
examples.explanation: These very active metals react with cold water, displace H from acids, and displace all metals below them.

image.caption: Most active metals react vigorously with water.

examples.process: Middle tier: Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Ni > Sn > Pb
examples.explanation: These metals react with acids to produce H₂; some react with steam; displace metals below them.

image.caption: These metals react with acids but not cold water.

examples.process: Bottom tier: (H) > Cu > Ag > Au > Pt
examples.explanation: These metals don't react with acids (except oxidizing acids); cannot displace H or metals above them.

image.caption: Least active metals resist oxidation.

categories.description: Halogens are ranked by their tendency to gain electrons (be reduced). Higher halogens displace lower halogens from salts.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Reactivity decreases down Group 17 due to increasing atomic size and decreasing electronegativity; F is strongest oxidizing agent.

examples.explanation: Fluorine displaces Cl, Br, I; chlorine displaces Br, I; bromine displaces I; iodine displaces none.

image.caption: Halogen reactivity decreases down the group.

examples.explanation: Chlorine (higher) displaces bromide ions; solution turns orange as Br₂ forms.

image.caption: Color change confirms halogen displacement.

examples.explanation: Iodine (lower) cannot displace chloride ions; no reaction occurs.

image.caption: Lower halogen cannot displace higher one.

categories.type: Using the Activity Series: Decision Flowchart
categories.description: A systematic approach to predict whether a single replacement reaction will occur.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Step 1: Identify the free element and the element in the compound. Step 2: Locate both in the appropriate activity series. Step 3: If free element is higher, reaction occurs; if lower, no reaction.

examples.explanation: Free element: Al; element in compound: Fe. Activity series: Al > Fe. Conclusion: Reaction occurs: $\text{2Al} + \text{3FeCl}_2 \rightarrow \text{2AlCl}_3 + \text{3Fe}$.

image.caption: Al is above Fe → reaction occurs.

examples.explanation: Free element: Ag; element in compound: Cu. Activity series: Cu > Ag. Conclusion: No reaction; Ag cannot displace Cu.

image.caption: Ag is below Cu → no reaction.

principles.statement: Hydrogen is included in the metal activity series as a reference: metals above H displace H from acids; metals below H do not.
principles.application: Zn (above H) + HCl → H₂ gas; Cu (below H) + HCl → no reaction. This explains why some metals react with acids and others don't.
principles.example: Hydrogen's position helps predict acid-metal reactivity without memorizing every combination.

principles.statement: The activity series correlates with standard reduction potentials: more negative E° values indicate greater tendency to oxidize (higher activity).
principles.application: Zn²⁺/Zn: E° = -0.76 V (active); Cu²⁺/Cu: E° = +0.34 V (less active). More negative = more reactive metal.
principles.analogy: Like a hill: metals with negative potentials 'roll downhill' easily (lose electrons); positive potentials resist rolling (hold electrons).

sections.section_title: Writing and Balancing Single Replacement Equations
sections.introduction: Once you predict that a reaction will occur, you must write correct formulas and balance the equation. This section provides a systematic approach.

key_concept.definition: Writing single replacement equations requires: (1) correct formulas for reactants and products, (2) proper state symbols, (3) balanced coefficients that conserve mass and charge.
key_concept.context: Common errors include incorrect ion charges, forgetting diatomic elements, and unbalanced equations that violate conservation laws.

process_flow.description: Determine if reaction is metal-metal, metal-acid, metal-water, or halogen-halogen replacement.
Verify that the free element is more reactive than the element it would replace. If not, write 'No Reaction'.
Write correct formulas for products: new compound (free element cation + original anion) and displaced element.
Assign (s), (l), (g), or (aq) based on physical states under reaction conditions.
Adjust coefficients to balance atoms and charge; verify conservation of mass.

worked_examples.title: Metal-Metal Replacement
worked_examples.problem: Write and balance the equation for the reaction between aluminum metal and aqueous copper(II) sulfate. Predict whether the reaction occurs.

step_by_step.explanation: Free element: Al(s); Compound: CuSO₄(aq). This is a metal-metal replacement.
Al is above Cu in activity series; Al is more reactive. Reaction will occur.
Al replaces Cu: Al forms Al³⁺; SO₄²⁻ remains. Product compound: Al₂(SO₄)₃. Displaced element: Cu(s).
Al(s) + CuSO₄(aq) → Al₂(SO₄)₃(aq) + Cu(s)
Balance SO₄ first (polyatomic ion): 3 CuSO₄ needed. Then Cu: 3 Cu on right. Then Al: 2 Al on left.

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{2Al}(s) + \text{3CuSO}_4(aq) \rightarrow \text{Al}_2(\text{SO}_4)_3(aq) + \text{3Cu}(s)$. Aluminum displaces copper because Al is more reactive. Blue Cu²⁺ solution fades; reddish Cu metal deposits.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Metal-Metal Replacement with Activity Series Prediction

worked_examples.title: Metal-Acid Replacement
worked_examples.problem: Will iron react with hydrochloric acid? If yes, write and balance the equation.

step_by_step.explanation: Free element: Fe(s); Compound: HCl(aq). This is metal-acid replacement (metal displacing H).
Fe is above H in activity series; Fe is more reactive than H. Reaction will occur.
Fe replaces H: Fe forms Fe²⁺ (common oxidation state); Cl⁻ remains. Product: FeCl₂. Displaced element: H₂(g) (diatomic).
Fe(s) + HCl(aq) → FeCl₂(aq) + H₂(g). Balance Cl: 2 HCl needed; H then balances.

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{Fe}(s) + \text{2HCl}(aq) \rightarrow \text{FeCl}_2(aq) + \text{H}_2(g)$. Iron reacts with hydrochloric acid because Fe is above H in the activity series. Bubbles of H₂ gas confirm the reaction.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Metal-Acid Replacement with Hydrogen Gas Production

worked_examples.title: Halogen-Halogen Replacement
worked_examples.problem: Write and balance the equation for chlorine gas bubbled through aqueous potassium iodide. Predict the observable change.

step_by_step.explanation: Free element: Cl₂(g); Compound: KI(aq). This is halogen-halogen replacement.
Cl is above I in halogen series; Cl₂ is more reactive. Reaction will occur.
Cl replaces I: Cl forms Cl⁻; K⁺ remains. Product: KCl. Displaced element: I₂ (diatomic; may appear as solid or in solution).
Cl₂(g) + KI(aq) → KCl(aq) + I₂. Balance I: 2 KI and I₂; then K and Cl balance with 2 KCl.
Colorless Cl₂ + colorless KI → colorless KCl + brown/purple I₂. Solution turns brown as iodine forms.

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{Cl}_2(g) + \text{2KI}(aq) \rightarrow \text{2KCl}(aq) + \text{I}_2(s)$. Chlorine displaces iodine because Cl is more reactive. The solution changes from colorless to brown/purple as iodine forms, providing visual confirmation of the reaction.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Halogen Displacement with Color Change Observation

worked_examples.title: No Reaction Prediction
worked_examples.problem: Will copper metal react with aqueous magnesium sulfate? Write the equation or explain why no reaction occurs.

step_by_step.explanation: Free element: Cu(s); Compound: MgSO₄(aq). Metal-metal replacement pattern.
Mg is above Cu in activity series; Cu is less reactive than Mg. Cu cannot displace Mg.
Since the free element (Cu) is less reactive than the element in the compound (Mg), no reaction occurs.

worked_examples.final_answer: No reaction occurs. Copper is below magnesium in the activity series, meaning Cu is less reactive and cannot displace Mg²⁺ from solution. The copper metal remains unchanged; the MgSO₄ solution remains colorless.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Activity Series Prediction of No Reaction

worked_examples.title: Metal-Water Replacement (Steam)
worked_examples.problem: Write and balance the equation for iron reacting with steam. Identify the products.

step_by_step.explanation: Free element: Fe(s); Compound: H₂O(g) (steam). Metal-water replacement.
Fe does not react with cold water but reacts with steam at high temperature. Reaction occurs under these conditions.
Fe displaces H from H₂O. With steam, Fe forms Fe₃O₄ (mixed oxide) or FeO; H₂ gas is produced. Common product: Fe₃O₄.
Fe + H₂O(g) → Fe₃O₄ + H₂. Balance Fe: 3 Fe; balance O: 4 H₂O; balance H: 4 H₂.

worked_examples.final_answer: $\text{3Fe}(s) + \text{4H}_2\text{O}(g) \xrightarrow{\text{high temp}} \text{Fe}_3\text{O}_4(s) + \text{4H}_2(g)$. Iron reacts with steam (not cold water) to form iron(II,III) oxide and hydrogen gas. This reaction requires high temperature.

sections.section_title: Real-World Applications of Single Replacement Reactions
sections.introduction: Single replacement reactions are not just textbook examples—they power important industrial processes, environmental phenomena, and everyday technologies.

key_concept.definition: Applications of single replacement reactions include metal extraction (hydrometallurgy), corrosion protection (sacrificial anodes), water treatment, and analytical chemistry tests.
key_concept.context: Understanding these reactions enables engineers and scientists to design processes that harness or prevent displacement chemistry.

categories.description: Less reactive metals are extracted from ores by displacement with more reactive metals in solution.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Metal ions in solution are reduced to elemental metal by a more active metal, which oxidizes and dissolves.

examples.explanation: Zinc displaces gold from cyanide complex in mining. Gold precipitates as pure metal; zinc dissolves.

image.caption: Zinc displacement recovers gold from solution.

examples.explanation: Scrap iron displaces copper from low-grade ore solutions. Economical method for copper recovery.

image.caption: Iron cementation recovers copper industrially.

categories.description: More active metals are attached to structures to corrode preferentially, protecting the less active metal.
categories.detailed_mechanism: The sacrificial anode (e.g., Zn, Mg) oxidizes instead of the protected metal (e.g., Fe), providing electrons that prevent corrosion.

examples.process: Galvanized steel: Zn coating on Fe
examples.explanation: If coating is scratched, Zn (more active) corrodes instead of Fe. Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻; Fe remains protected.

image.caption: Zinc sacrificially protects iron from rusting.

examples.explanation: Magnesium blocks attached to steel hulls corrode preferentially in seawater, preventing ship rusting.

image.caption: Sacrificial anodes protect marine structures.

categories.description: Reactive metals displace toxic heavy metals from contaminated water, precipitating them for removal.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Added metal (e.g., Fe) reduces toxic metal ions (e.g., Cr⁶⁺, Hg²⁺) to less soluble or less toxic forms.

examples.explanation: Iron reduces toxic Cr(VI) to less toxic Cr(III), which precipitates as Cr(OH)₃ for removal.

image.caption: Iron reduces toxic chromium for safe disposal.

examples.explanation: Iron displaces mercury from solution; liquid mercury is collected and safely handled.

image.caption: Displacement precipitates toxic mercury.

categories.description: Single replacement reactions identify unknown metals or halogens by observing displacement and color changes.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Known reagents displace unknown elements; observations (color, precipitate, gas) identify the unknown.

examples.process: Halide test: Cl₂ water + unknown salt
examples.explanation: If solution turns orange/brown, Br⁻ or I⁻ was present (displaced by Cl₂). Further tests distinguish Br⁻ from I⁻.

image.caption: Color changes identify halide ions.

examples.process: Metal activity test: Unknown metal + CuSO₄
examples.explanation: If reddish Cu deposits, unknown metal is above Cu in activity series. No deposit means metal is below Cu.

image.caption: Displacement tests rank metal reactivity.

principles.statement: A more active metal will oxidize preferentially to protect a less active metal; this is the basis of cathodic protection.
principles.application: Zn coating on Fe (galvanizing), Mg anodes on ships, Al on steel structures—all use single replacement chemistry to prevent corrosion.
principles.analogy: Like a bodyguard: the sacrificial metal 'takes the hit' (corrodes) so the protected metal stays safe.

principles.statement: The activity series enables selective recovery of valuable metals from complex mixtures by choosing appropriate displacing agents.
principles.application: Zn recovers Au from cyanide solution but not Fe; Fe recovers Cu from solution but not Zn. Selectivity enables purification.
principles.example: This principle is essential for sustainable metal recycling and resource recovery.

sections.introduction: Avoid these frequent errors when working with single replacement reactions; apply expert strategies for accurate predictions and equations.

key_concept.definition: Common single replacement errors include misapplying the activity series, incorrect ion charges, forgetting diatomic elements, and balancing mistakes with polyatomic ions.
key_concept.context: Developing careful habits in reactivity assessment and equation writing prevents persistent errors in displacement problems.

characteristics: Writing equations for reactions that don't occur
Not checking relative reactivity first
Predicting products for impossible reactions
Wasting time balancing non-reactions
classifications.behavior: This error leads to chemically impossible equations and incorrect predictions in lab or industry.

examples.explanation: Cu is below Zn in activity series; cannot displace Zn. Correct: No Reaction.

image.caption: Always check activity series before writing equation.

characteristics: Writing FeCl instead of FeCl₂ or FeCl₃
Not knowing common ion charges for transition metals
Confusing Roman numeral in name with subscript
Invalid compound formulas
classifications.behavior: Incorrect formulas represent non-existent compounds; the reaction equation is meaningless.

examples.explanation: Iron forms Fe²⁺ (or Fe³⁺), not Fe⁺. Correct: Fe + 2HCl → FeCl₂ + H₂.

image.caption: Know common oxidation states: Fe²⁺, Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺, etc.

classifications.value: Writing H, Cl, Br, I Instead of H₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂
characteristics: Writing H instead of H₂ for hydrogen gas
Using Cl instead of Cl₂ for chlorine
Incorrect atom counts in balancing
Fundamental formula error
classifications.behavior: This error makes equations chemically incorrect and impossible to balance properly.

examples.explanation: Hydrogen gas is diatomic: H₂, not H. Correct: Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl₂ + H₂.

image.caption: Remember: H₂, N₂, O₂, F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂ are diatomic.

classifications.type: Pro Tip: Use the Activity Series Checklist
characteristics: Identify free element and element in compound ✓
Locate both in correct activity series ✓
Free element higher? → Reaction; lower? → No Reaction ✓
Predict products only if reaction occurs ✓
classifications.behavior: This systematic check prevents writing equations for impossible reactions.

examples.explanation: Free: Al; in compound: Cu. Series: Al > Cu. Conclusion: Reaction occurs → predict products → balance.

image.caption: Checklist ensures logical prediction workflow.

classifications.type: Pro Tip: Memorize Key Activity Series Segments
characteristics: Top metals (Li-K-Ba-Ca-Na): react with cold water
Middle metals (Mg-Al-Zn-Fe): react with acids, not cold water
Hydrogen reference: above H reacts with acids; below H doesn't
Bottom metals (Cu-Ag-Au): noble, resist oxidation
classifications.behavior: Knowing these segments enables quick reactivity assessments without consulting a full chart.

examples.explanation: Ni is in middle tier (above H); yes, reacts: Ni + 2HCl → NiCl₂ + H₂.

image.caption: Memorized segments speed prediction.

principles.statement: To identify a single replacement reaction: look for one element + one compound → new element + new compound (A + BC → AC + B).
principles.application: Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu (single replacement); Zn + H₂SO₄ → ZnSO₄ + H₂ (also single replacement, H is 'element' in acid).
principles.analogy: Like musical chairs: one player (element) takes another's seat (in compound), leaving the displaced player free.

principles.statement: Every single replacement reaction is a redox process: the replacing element is oxidized (loses electrons), the displaced element is reduced (gains electrons).
principles.example: Recognizing the redox nature helps balance equations using half-reactions in advanced chemistry.

summary: Single replacement (displacement) reactions follow the pattern A + BC → AC + B, where a more reactive element displaces a less reactive element from a compound. These reactions are governed by the activity series: for metals, reactivity is the tendency to lose electrons (oxidize); for halogens, reactivity is the tendency to gain electrons (be reduced). An element can displace any element below it in the series but not those above. Types include: metal-metal displacement (Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu), metal-acid displacement producing H₂ gas (Mg + 2HCl → MgCl₂ + H₂), metal-water displacement (2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂), and halogen-halogen displacement (Cl₂ + 2NaBr → 2NaCl + Br₂). All single replacement reactions are redox processes. Key skills include: using the activity series to predict reaction spontaneity, writing correct formulas with proper ion charges, remembering diatomic elements (H₂, Cl₂, etc.), balancing equations with polyatomic ions treated as units, and recognizing 'No Reaction' when the free element is less reactive. Applications include metal extraction (hydrometallurgy), corrosion protection (sacrificial anodes), water treatment, and analytical tests. Common mistakes include ignoring the activity series, using incorrect ion charges, and forgetting diatomic elements. Mastery of single replacement reactions enables prediction and control of important chemical processes in industry, environmental science, and materials engineering.
